name: Begonia soli-mutata
origin: Southeastern Brazil (humid forest floor, shaded understory)
aboutText: Begonia soli-mutata, described by L.B.Smith and Schubert, is a rare rhizomatous species native to the humid forests of southeastern Brazil — growing on the forest floor in warm, shaded, consistently moist conditions, creeping through leaf litter and humus-rich soil in the sheltered understory. The name 'soli-mutata' (sun-changed) refers to a remarkable property: the leaf colouration shifts in response to light intensity, with the dark emerald to olive-green surface tones altering perceptibly as conditions change — a response that makes the plant appear slightly different at different times of day. The texture reinforces the drama: leaves are strongly bullate and quilted, asymmetrical and rounded to ovate, with deep blistered zones and contrasting lime-green venation and banding, while the undersides are rich red to burgundy. A creeping rhizome spreads along the forest floor rooting at nodes, creating low colonies of exceptional foliage.

morphology.leafShape: Asymmetrical, rounded to ovate, strongly bullate and quilted
morphology.texture: Strongly bullate, deep blistered texture, red to burgundy undersides
morphology.variegation: None — natural bicolour of dark emerald and lime-green venation; colour shifts with light
morphology.growthHabit: Creeping rhizome spreading along forest floor, rooting at nodes

propagation.notes: Most rhizomatous begonias root readily from leaf sections or petiole cuttings placed on moist substrate. Keep warm and humid until plantlets emerge at leaf margins.

careGuide.substrate: Light, well-draining mix: 50% perlite, 30% potting compost, 20% orchid bark. Begonias are susceptible to root rot — excellent drainage is essential.
careGuide.watering: Allow the top half of the substrate to dry between waterings. Water at the base — wet leaves invite fungal problems. Reduce in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70%. More tolerant of average indoor humidity than tropical aroids, but consistent moisture prevents crispy leaf margins.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced liquid fertiliser at quarter strength every 2–3 weeks during the growing season. Do not feed in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months in spring. Begonias like shallow, wide pots that suit rhizomatous growth. Don't bury the rhizome.

commonProblems.cause: Poor air circulation combined with high humidity
commonProblems.fix: Improve airflow; avoid wetting leaves; treat with a dilute bicarbonate of soda spray

commonProblems.cause: Overwatering, dead leaves left on plant, poor air circulation
commonProblems.fix: Remove dead foliage promptly; increase airflow; reduce watering
Move to a brighter position — begonias need good indirect light to maintain compact growth

items: Check for powdery mildew — a white dusty coating on leaf surfaces, especially in dry conditions; it spreads rapidly between plants
Inspect for mealybugs at leaf axils and the underside of stems where they hide in white cottony deposits
Rhizome begonias should show firm, healthy rhizome tissue — avoid plants with soft, discoloured rhizomes
Most begonias ship well as leaf cuttings — if buying a cutting, ensure it has a healthy petiole and no signs of rot at the cut end

fieldNotes.title: Colour That Evolves
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: A southeastern Brazilian begonia with a remarkable characteristic: the leaves change colour as they mature, starting bronze or reddish-purple on emergence and settling to dark green with bold veining at maturity. Sol-mutata references this solar colour change. From shaded humid forest floor with the typical requirements of Brazilian Atlantic Forest specialists: consistent humidity, good drainage, indirect light. The colour change over leaf maturation gives the plant a dynamic quality that static-leaf species lack — you are effectively growing two plants at once depending on which leaf age you are observing. An interesting one.
